title: Graphs
table_of_contents: The implementation with adjacency linked lists

items.text:
• G = (V , E )
• V a set of vertices
• E a set of edges; an edge = a non-ordered pair of distinct vertices

0,1 - the ends of u
u is incident with 0 and 1
0 and 1 are adjacent (neighbors)

• Walk from u to v : u = i0, {i0, i1}, i1, · · · , {ik−1, ik }, ik = v
3, {3,2}, 2, {2,0}, 0, {0,1}, 1, {1,3},3, {3,2},2
• Trail: a walk where any two edges are distinct
• Path: a walk where any two vertices are distinct
• Cycle: closed walk (i0 = ik )
• Circuit = a closed walk where any two intermediate vertices are
distinct

• i R j if and only if there is a path from i to j
• R is an equivalence relation
• V1, · · · , Vp equivalence classes
• Gi = (Vi , Ei ) a subgraph induced by Vi
• G1, · · · , Gp – connected components
• connected graph = a graph with a single connected component

• objects:
• graphs G = (V , E ), V = {0, 1, · · · , n − 1}
• operations:
• emptyGraph()
• input: nothing
• output: the empty graph (∅, ∅)
• isEmptyGraph()
• input: G = (V , E ),
• output: true if G = (∅, ∅), false other way
• insertEdge()
• input: G = (V , E ), i, j ∈ V
• output: G = (V , E ∪ {i, j})
• insertVertex()
• input: G = (V , E ), V = {0, 1, · · · , n − 1}
• output: G = (V (cid:48), E ), V (cid:48) = {0, 1, · · · , n − 1, n}

• removeEdge()
• input: G = (V , E ), i, j ∈ V
• output: G = (V , E –{i, j})
• removeVertex()
• input: G = (V , E ), V = {0, 1, · · · , n − 1}, k
• output: G = (V (cid:48), E (cid:48)), V (cid:48) = {0, 1, · · · , n − 2}
{i (cid:48), j (cid:48)} ∈ E (cid:48) ⇔ (∃{i, j} ∈ E ) i (cid:54)= k, j (cid:54)= k,
i (cid:48) = if (i < k) then i else i − 1,
j (cid:48) = if (j < k) then j else j − 1

• adjacencyList()
• input: G = (V , E ), i ∈ V
• output: the list of vertices adjacent with i
• listOfReachableVertices()
• input: G = (V , E ), i ∈ V
• output: the list of vertices reachable from i

• D = (V , A)
• V a set of vertices
• A a set of arcs (directed edges); an arc = an ordered pair of distinct
vertices

0 – the tail of a
1 – the head of a

• Walk: i0, (i0, i1), i1, · · · , (ik−1, ik ), ik
3, (3,2), 2, (2,0), 0, (0,1), 1, (1,2), 2, (2,0), 0
• Trail: a walk where any two edges are distinct
• Path: a walk where any two vertices are distinct
• Cycle: closed walk (i0 = ik )
• Circuit = a closed walk where any two intermediate vertices are
distinct

• i R j if and only if there is a path from i to j and a path from j to i
• R is an equivalence relation
• V1, · · · , Vp the equivalence classes
• Gi = (Vi , Ai ) the subdigraph induced by Vi
• G1, · · · , Gp – strongly connected components
• digraph strongly connected = digraph with a single strongly
connected component

• objects: digraphs D = (V , A)
• operations:
• emptyDigraph()
• input: nothing
• output: the empty digraph (∅, ∅)
• isEmptyDigraph()
• input: D = (V , A),
• output: true if D = (∅, ∅), false other way
• insertArc()
• input: D = (V , A), i, j ∈ V
• output: D = (V , A ∪ (i, j))
• insertVertex()
• input: D = (V , A), V = {0, 1, · · · , n − 1}
• output: D = (V (cid:48), A), V (cid:48) = {0, 1, · · · , n − 1, n}

• removeArc()
• input: D = (V , A), i, j ∈ V
• output: D = (V , A–(i, j))
• removeVertex()
• input: D = (V , A), V = {0, 1, · · · , n − 1}, k
• output: D = (V (cid:48), A(cid:48)), V (cid:48) = {0, 1, · · · , n − 2}
{i (cid:48), j (cid:48)} ∈ A(cid:48) ⇔ (∃{i, j} ∈ A) i (cid:54)= k, j (cid:54)= k,
i (cid:48) = if (i < k) then i else i − 1,
j (cid:48) = if (j < k) then j else j − 1

• outAdjacencyList()
• input: D = (V , A), i ∈ V
• output: the list of direct successors of i
• inAdjacencyList()
• input: D = (V , A), i ∈ V
• output: the list of direct predecessors of i
• listOfReachableVertices()
• input: D = (V , A), i ∈ V
• output: the list of vertices reachable from i

content_blocks.title: The representation of graphs as digraphs

items.text:
• the topology is preserved
• the adjacency list of i in G = the out (=in) adjacency list of i in D

sections.title: The implementation of digraphs with adjacency matrices

content_blocks.title: The implementation of digraphs with adjacency matrices

items.text:
• the representation of digraphs
• n the number of vertices
• m the number of arcs (optional)
• a matrix (a[i, j]| 1 ≤ i, j ≤ n)
a[i, j] = if (i, j) ∈ A then 1 else 0
• if the digraph is a graph, then a[i, j] is symmetric
• the out adjacency list of i ⊆ line i
• the in adjacency list of i ⊆ column i

• operations
• emptyDigraph
n ← 0; m ← 0
• insertVertex: O(n)
• insertArc: O(1)
• removeArc: O(1)

• removeVertex()
Procedure removeVertex(a, n, k)
begin
for i ← 0 to n − 1 do
for j ← 0 to n − 1 do
if (i > k) then
a[i − 1, j] ← a[i, j]
if (j > k) then

a[i, j − 1] ← a[i, j]
n ← n − 1
end
the execution time: O(n2)

• listOfReachableVertices()
Procedure reflTransClosure(a, n, b) // (Warshall, 1962)
begin
for i ← 0 to n − 1 do
for j ← 0 to n − 1 do
b[i, j] ← a[i, j]
if (i = j) then

b[i, j] ← 1
for k ← 0 to n − 1 do
for i ← 0 to n − 1 do
if (b[i, k] = 1) then
for j ← 0 to n − 1 do
if (b[k, j] = 1) then
b[i, j] ← 1
end
the execution time: O(n3)

• the representation of digraphs with adjacency lists
• a vector a[0..n − 1] of linked lists (pointers)
• a[i] is the out adjacency list corresponding to i

• operations
• emptyDigraph
• insertVertex: O(1)
• insertArc: O(1)
• removeVertex: O(n + m)
• removeArc: O(m)

• it manages two sets
• S = the set of already visited vertices
• SB ⊆ S the subset of vertices for which there are chances to find
neighbors not visited yet
• the adjacency list of i is divided in:

• the current step
• read a vertex i from SB
• extract j from the ”waiting” list of i (if it is nonempty)
• if j isn’t in S, then add it to S and to SB
• if the ”waiting” list of i is empty, then remove i from SB
• initially
• S = SB = {i0}
• the ”waiting” list of i = the adjacency list of i
• termination SB = ∅

Procedure exploration(a, n, i0, S)
begin
for i ← 0 to n − 1 do
p[i] ← a[i]
SB ← (i0)
visit(i0); S ← (i0)
while (SB (cid:54)= ∅) do
i ← read(SB)
if (p[i] = NULL) then
SB ← SB − {i}
else
j ← p[i] → varf
p[i] ← p[i] → succ
if (j (cid:54)∈ S) then
SB ← SB ∪ {j}
visit(j); S ← S ∪ {j}
end

Theorem
Assuming that the operations over S and SB as well as visit() are
achieved in O(1), the time complexity, in the worst case, of the
exploration algorithm is O(n + m).

• SB is implemented as a stack
SB ← (i0) ⇔SB ← emptyStack()
push(SB, i0)
i ← read(SB) ⇔i ← top(SB)
SB ← SB − {i} ⇔pop(SB)
SB ← SB ∪ {j} ⇔push(SB, j)

• SB is implemented as a queue
SB ← (i0) ⇔SB ← emptyQueue();
insert(SB, i0)
i ← read(SB) ⇔read(SB, i)
SB ← SB − {i} ⇔remove(SB)
SB ← SB ∪ {j} ⇔insert(SB, j)

Function ConnectedCompDFS(D)
begin
for i ← 0 to n − 1 do
color [i] ← 0
k ← 0
for i ← 0 to n − 1 do
if (color [i] = 0) then
k ← k + 1
DfsRecConnectedComp(i, k)
return k
end

Procedure DfsRecConnectedComp(i, k)
begin
color [i] ← k
for (each vertex j in listaDeAdiac(i)) do
if (color [j] = 0) then
DfsRecConnectedComp(j, k)
end

Procedure DfsStronglyConnectedComp(D)
begin
for i ← 0 to n − 1 do
color [i] ← 0
parent[i] ← −1
time ← 0
for i ← 0 to n − 1 do
if (color [i] = 0) then
DfsRecStronglyConnectedComp(i)
end

Procedure DfsRecStronglyConnectedComp(i)
begin
time ← time + 1
color [i] ← 1
for (each vertex j in adiacList(i)) do
if (color [j] = 0) then
parent[j] ← i
DfsRecStronglyConnectedComp(j)
time ← time + 1
finalTime[i] ← time
end

Notation: D T = (V , AT ), (i, j) ∈ A ⇔ (j, i) ∈ AT
Procedure StronglyConnectedComp(D)
begin
1. DFSStronglyConnectedComp(D)
2. compute D T
3. DFSStronglyConnectedComp(D T ) but considering in the for main
loop the vertices in descending order of their final times of visiting
finalTime[i]
4. return each tree computed at step 3 as being a distinct strongly
connected component
end

content_blocks.title: Finding the strongly connected components: complexity

items.text:
• DFSStronglyConnectedComp(D): O(n + m)
• compute D T : O(m)
• DFSStronglyConnectedComp(D T ): O(n + m)
• Total: O(n + m)

• Algorithms, path problems, computer networks (routing), genomics
(alignment networks, genome assembly), multi-relational data mining,
operations research (scheduling), artificial intelligence (constraint
satisfaction), etc.

The Konigsberg Bridge Problem (1736): starting from one land masses,
walk over each of the seven bridges just once
The land masses: vertices, the bridges: edges
It is possible to choose a vertex, proceed along the edges and return to the
chosen vertex, covering each edge once?

• Google search engine: PageRank algorithm - to determine how
important a given web page is
• Geographic Information Systems (GIS): Google Maps, Bing Maps
• Social networks

• The design of DNA codes that satisfy combinatorial constraints; use
in biomolecular computing to store information, or to manipulate
molecules in chemical libraries
• Find the largest set S of strings of length n over the alphabet
{A, C , G , T } s.t.:

• GC Content Constraint: each word has 50% symbols from {C , G }
• Hamming Distance Constraint: each pair of words, w1 (cid:54)= w2 differ in at
least d positions: H(w1, w2) ≥ d
• Reverse Complement Hamming Distance Constraint:
H(R(w1), C (w2)) ≥ d; R(w ): the reversed of word w and C (w ) the
complement of w (C ↔ G , A ↔ T )

• every DNA word has an assigned vertex vi
• E = EHD ∪ ERC (EHD pairs of words with a HD conflict, ERC pairs of
words with a RC conflict)
• a solution: a maximum independent set
Figura: Graphs for words of size 2 and Hamming distance d = 2 (left) and d = 3
(right)
